passages.explanation: One day as Jesus is teaching and proclaiming the good news in the temple courts, the chief priests, teachers of the law, and elders confront him: tell us by what authority you're doing these things. Who gave you this authority? Jesus replies with a question: John's baptism — was it from heaven, or of human origin? They discuss it among themselves: if we say from heaven, he'll ask why we didn't believe John. If we say of human origin, all the people will stone us because they're persuaded John was a prophet. They answer: we don't know. Jesus says: neither will I tell you by what authority I do these things. Jesus doesn't dodge the question — he exposes their bad faith. They won't answer honestly, so an honest answer from him would be wasted. Their inability to evaluate John disqualifies them from evaluating Jesus.

Jesus tells the people a parable: a man plants a vineyard, rents it to tenants, and goes away for a long time. At harvest he sends a servant to the tenants for some of the fruit. They beat the servant and send him away empty-handed. He sends another — they beat and treat him shamefully. He sends a third — they wound him and throw him out. The owner says: what shall I do? I'll send my son, whom I love — perhaps they'll respect him. But when the tenants see him, they plot: this is the heir — let's kill him, and the inheritance will be ours. They throw him out of the vineyard and kill him. What then will the owner do? He'll come and kill those tenants and give the vineyard to others. When the people hear this they say: God forbid! Jesus looks at them: then what is the meaning of the scripture — the stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone? Everyone who falls on that stone will be broken to pieces; anyone on whom it falls will be crushed. The teachers of the law and the chief priests look for a way to arrest him immediately — they know he's told this parable against them. But they fear the people. The vineyard is Israel. The tenants are the religious leaders. The servants are the prophets. The son is Jesus. The parable is a compressed history of Israel's rejection of God's messengers, building to the final, fatal rejection.

Keeping a close watch on Jesus, they send spies who pretend to be sincere. They hope to catch him in something he says so they can hand him over to the governor. They ask: teacher, we know you speak and teach what is right. Is it right for us to pay taxes to Caesar or not? Jesus sees through their duplicity: show me a denarius. Whose image and inscription are on it? Caesar's, they reply. He says: then give back to Caesar what is Caesar's, and to God what is God's. They're unable to trap him and are astonished by his answer, so they become silent. The trap is perfect: say yes to taxes and lose the crowd; say no and face Roman charges. Jesus' answer transcends both options — Caesar's coin bears Caesar's image, but human beings bear God's image. Give each what belongs to them.

Some Sadducees, who deny the resurrection, pose a hypothetical: Moses wrote that if a man's brother dies leaving a wife but no children, the brother must marry the widow and raise children for him. Seven brothers successively marry the same woman and die childless. In the resurrection, whose wife will she be? Jesus answers: the people of this age marry and are given in marriage. But those considered worthy of the age to come and the resurrection from the dead will neither marry nor be given in marriage. They can no longer die, for they are like the angels. They are God's children, since they are children of the resurrection. Even Moses showed that the dead rise — in the burning bush passage he calls the Lord the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. He is not the God of the dead, but of the living — for to him all are alive. Some teachers of the law respond: well said, teacher. And no one dares ask him any more questions. Jesus demolishes the argument by showing the Sadducees misunderstand both the nature of resurrection life and their own Scriptures. God's present-tense relationship with the patriarchs implies their continued existence.

Jesus asks them: why is it said that the Messiah is the son of David? David himself declares in the Book of Psalms: the Lord said to my Lord, sit at my right hand until I make your enemies a footstool for your feet. David calls him Lord — so how can he be his son? The Messiah is David's descendant but also David's Lord — a paradox that only makes sense if the Messiah is both human and divine. Then, in the hearing of all the people, Jesus warns his disciples: beware of the teachers of the law. They like to walk around in flowing robes and love to be greeted with respect in the marketplaces and have the most important seats in the synagogues and the places of honour at banquets. They devour widows' houses and for a show make lengthy prayers. These men will be punished most severely. Jesus connects their theological authority directly to their moral failure. The people who should know God best are exploiting the vulnerable and performing piety for status.
